section_title: Credit: Reviewed credit portfolios and bolstered reserves
section_statement: TD Bank strengthened its credit reserves by $500MM specifically to address potential tariff impacts, while maintaining disciplined risk assessment across its loan portfolio. Despite tariff uncertainties, underlying credit quality remains strong with watch lists improving across all business segments.

quotes.quote_text: First are the macro changes, determined by our chief economist. They're not determined by me, but we have a strong governance process and we take it through our governance process.
We did put tariffs into these scenarios:
quotes.context: For Canada: tariffs of 10% for six months, then those come down over a period of time For the U.S.: tariffs of 12% for six months, then those come down over a period of time

quotes.quote_text: Our downside case is a recessionary case. And that downside case also reflects the risks of higher tariffs. In addition to the macro, what we've done is we've looked more holistically at the book and said at higher tariffs rate what could be the impact on consumers and non-consumers and that's the way we built the overlay. So, it's really a combination of these macro plus the judgmental overlay that got us to the $500MM number [business reserve build].
quotes.context: Reserve allocation strategy: Built reserves for consumer, business, and government lending. Expected bigger impact on non-retail sectors. Most of the $500MM reserve build has been allocated to business and government lending. If macro conditions deteriorate, consumer impact is also expected.
quotes.sentiment_rationale: Proactive risk management with significant reserve building

quotes.quote_text: We looked at the industries that were most exposed to tariffs. What we did is we went actually within those industries borrow by borrower and assessed what is the financial impact on the borrower. So for example we'd look at revenue we'd look at cost of goods sold and we'd look at what impact that would have on the ratings of that individual.
quotes.context: Key Exposure Metrics: - Gross loans to industries most exposed to policy and trade uncertainty: ~9% of Total Bank gross loans - Loans to borrowers most sensitive to policy and trade actions: <1% of Total Bank gross loans

quotes.quote_text: The 9% number that we have disclosed, we went and looked on a more narrow basis saying what out of these borrowers are most sensitive to tariffs. And, we looked broadly at the financial impact, again, across the 9%, not just the smaller population. We looked at the ratings migration that could occur, and based on that ratings migration, we built our reserves. There is some for consumer because if the macro deteriorates, I think you should expect there'll be impact on the consumer as well.
quotes.sentiment_rationale: Comprehensive risk evaluation with consumer impact consideration

quotes.quote_text: Our reserves reflect a forward-looking view as at April 30. So, if that forward-looking view evolves we may have to build more reserves.
The watch list has evolved positively and has been coming down across all business segments.
Current impaired PCL: $946 million (decrease of $270 million quarter-over-quarter)
quotes.context:
Largest Quarter-over-Quarter Decreases by Asset Class:
- US Cards portfolios: Related to seasonal trends coupled with impact of prior quarter model update
- US Commercial lending portfolio

quotes.quote_text: If you keep this tariff issue aside, we were really seeing peak PCL and good quality, and I think the fact that rates have come down have helped borrowers.
On a broader basis, we're seeing good credit quality. On wholesale, numbers went up a little bit... So that's the overall picture on impaired PCL, and I do view it as quite a positive development.
quotes.context: Wholesale PCL increase: $28 million Drivers: Incremental PCL for three existing borrowers in: - Telco - Media - Power & Utility - CRE Office Additional impacts: Two impairments in forestry sector

quotes.quote_text: But again, for this year as like I'd really ask you to anchor on the fact that we're not changing our guidance. We're still saying for this year 45 basis points to 55 basis points but we're also calling out that the risk to that range is elevated because of the environment.
quotes.sentiment_rationale: Maintains guidance but flags elevated risks

quotes.quote_text: We expect tariff resolution to be somewhere in Q3 and if that it takes longer and that would worsen the economic outlook and potentially drive higher PCL.
